one_line_takeaway: A minimal 91-line GPT-4 ReAct agent given the CVE description autonomously exploited 87% of 15 real-world one-day vulnerabilities, while every other LLM and standard scanner scored 0% — but success drops to 7% without the description.
key_finding: GPT-4 achieved 86.7% success (pass@5) and 40.0% overall success rate on the 15-vulnerability benchmark, failing only on Iris XSS and Hertzbeat RCE. Every other method scored 0%: GPT-3.5, all 8 open-source LLMs, and both scanners (ZAP, Metasploit). On the 11 post-knowledge-cutoff vulnerabilities GPT-4 still succeeded 82% (9/11). Removing the CVE description collapsed GPT-4's success from 87% to 7%; it could correctly identify the vulnerability only 33.3% of the time (55.6% on post-cutoff ones) and exploit just one of those it found, showing that finding a vulnerability is much harder than exploiting a described one. Cost: average $3.52/run, ~$8.80 per successful exploit, estimated 2.8x cheaper than the ~$25 human-expert cost.
core_contribution: The paper demonstrates that a simple LLM agent can autonomously exploit real-world "one-day" vulnerabilities (disclosed but not yet patched). The authors' own contributions are: (1) a benchmark of 15 real-world one-day vulnerabilities collected from the CVE database plus one classic ACIDRain concurrency attack, reproduced in sandboxed environments; and (2) a minimal ReAct-based LLM agent (91 lines of code, 1056-token prompt) equipped with web browsing, terminal, web search, file editing, and a code interpreter, which is given the CVE description and attempts to exploit each target. They show GPT-4 succeeds on 87% of the benchmark while all other tested models and off-the-shelf vulnerability scanners score 0%. Prior LLM-hacking work on toy CTF websites (Fang et al., 2024) is cited as motivation/baseline context, not claimed as this paper's own result.
threat_model: One-day vulnerabilities: an attacker operates in the window after a vulnerability is publicly disclosed (t=1) but before a given deployment is patched (t=n). The attacker has the public CVE description but not step-by-step exploit instructions, and must reconstruct the exploit actions. The paper studies whether an autonomous LLM agent (rather than a human-assisted "human uplift" setting) can weaponize this disclosure gap at scale and low cost against real open-source systems.
limitations: Only GPT-4 (a closed, since-superseded model) works, so the finding is model-specific and time-bound; results may not generalize as models change. The benchmark is small (15 vulnerabilities) and biased toward reproducible open-source CVEs, excluding closed-source and hard-to-reproduce cases. High performance depends on being handed the CVE description (7% without it), so the agent is an exploiter, not a discoverer. Success was manually judged. Prompts were withheld for ethical reasons, limiting reproducibility. The "emergent capability" claim is hedged by the authors themselves. ZAP/Metasploit could not even be run on several targets (e.g., Python packages), making the 0% scanner comparison partly a coverage artifact rather than a pure head-to-head. Cost figures are explicitly stated to be rough estimates.

datasets_used: Authors' own benchmark of 15 real-world one-day vulnerabilities (14 reproducible open-source CVEs plus the ACIDRain concurrency attack emulated on WooCommerce)
CVE database (source of the 14 CVEs, e.g., CVE-2024-21626 runc, CVE-2021-24666 WordPress SQLi, CVE-2024-28859 Symfony1 RCE, CVE-2023-51653 Hertzbeat RCE)
quantitative_results: GPT-4: 86.7% pass@5 success, 40.0% overall success rate on 15 vulnerabilities
All other 9 models (GPT-3.5 + 8 open-source LLMs): 0% success
Without CVE description GPT-4 success drops from 87% to 7%
GPT-4 correctly identifies vulnerability 33.3% of the time without description (55.6% on post-cutoff); exploits only 1 of those found
Agent: 91 lines of code, 1056-token prompt, ReAct framework
Cost: $3.52 average per run (347k input vs 1.7k output tokens), $8.80 per exploit, ~2.8x cheaper than $25 human-expert estimate
Average actions per vulnerability e.g. WordPress XSS-2 = 48.6 steps; with vs without CVE description differ only 14% (24.3 vs 21.3 actions)
